mappings.rationaleAI: Definitions and ranges of normality in human health (B) and regimes affecting standard values including nutrition, exercise, and sleep (D).
Fertilization as the beginning of life (A.1), prenatal development through embryonic and fetal stages (A.2), and birth (A.3).
Functions of food (D.1.a), classes of food providing macro- and micronutrients (D.1.b), and recommended intakes of nutrients (D.1.c).
The nature of sleep including criteria for defining sleep (D.3.a) and psychophysiological variations including REM, NREM, and dreaming (D.3.b).
Dying and death as the final stage in the human life cycle (A.7).
Aging as a cumulative biological process constituting a stage in the human life cycle (A.6).
Exercise needs for health maintenance and avoidance of injuries (D.2.a) and physiological responses to exercise and effects of physical conditioning (D.2.b).
Obesity as a condition related to feeding behaviour (D.1.d) manageable through therapeutic diets and lifestyle modification (D.1.e).
Infancy as a stage of postnatal development covering sensorimotor responsiveness and early language acquisition (A.4).
Allergy as a hypersensitivity disorder involving the immune response as a bodily mechanism for stress maintenance (C.2.d).
Vaccines conferring active acquired immunity as part of the immune response defense mechanism against disease (C.2.d).
Injury triggering inflammatory responses and wound healing as part of regeneration and tissue repair processes (C.4).
Blood's role in the prevention of hemorrhage through clotting mechanisms (C.3).
Bodily defenses against disease including skin integrity, phagocytic cells, inflammation, and immune response (C.2).
Adolescence as a stage of postnatal development involving puberty-driven physical and cognitive changes (A.4).
Childhood as a stage of postnatal development involving progressive maturation (A.4).
Parenting encompassing physical, cognitive, and emotional support during postnatal development stages (A.4).
Food as substances consumed for nutritional support, relevant to functions of food in maintaining health (D.1.a).
Meat as a class of food providing protein and fat as part of nutritional classes of food (D.1.b).
Drinking water as essential for nutrition and hydration, relevant to nutrition and diet (D.1).
Alcoholic beverages as substances affecting feeding behaviour and health through CNS depressant effects (D.1.d).
Pasteur's germ theory establishing the basis for understanding bodily defenses against disease (C.2).
Physiology as the study of normal function, relevant to definitions and ranges of normality in human health (B).
The human body's homeostatic mechanisms for maintenance of the internal environment during stress (C.1).
Swimming as a form of exercise providing cardiovascular conditioning and health benefits (D.2).
Diabetes management involving therapeutic diets and lifestyle interventions (D.1.e).
Salt as a dietary source of sodium and chloride electrolytes, a class of food (D.1.b) with recommended intake limits (D.1.c).
Bread as a baked staple providing carbohydrates as a class of food (D.1.b).
Fruit as a food class providing vitamins, minerals, and dietary fiber (D.1.b).
Beverages as liquids for human consumption relevant to feeding behaviour and hydration (D.1.d).
